name: Ruy Lopez
description: The Spanish: 3.Bb5 — closed main lines and the Exchange variation.
source: curated repertoire (scripts/repertoires/ruy-lopez.pgn) + lichess explorer, ratings 1400/1600/1800, speeds blitz/rapid

children.comment: Stop ...Bg4: the d4 break should not cost the f3 knight.
The bishop gets c2, the d4 break gets support: the whole Spanish plan in one pawn move.
Protect e4 first: only now is the ...b5 and ...Nxe4 fork trick harmless.
The Open: recover the pawn with the initiative.
A pure structure battle: your kingside majority is real, Black's crippled queenside one is not.
Better than 5.Nxe5?! Qd4 which regains the pawn at once; castle and play d4 on your terms.
The same slow build as the Italian: Re1, Nf1-g3, d4 when ready.
The Spanish bishop retreats behind its own rook: e4 is solid and every White piece stands better than its counterpart.
Full centre, easy play; Black defends a cramped Philidor.
Simple development; the open f-file cuts both ways and Black's king has nowhere safe.
Decline calmly: protect e4, develop, and Black's weakened king remains for the whole game.

variations.name: Closed main line
variations.intro: The Spanish torture: pressure on e5 and the queenside, the Bb3-c2 regrouping, and the great central break d4.

variations.name: Exchange variation
variations.intro: 4.Bxc6: trade the bishop, damage the pawns, and aim for any endgame - the four-vs-three kingside majority wins them.

variations.name: Berlin with 4.d3
variations.intro: Against the Berlin 3...Nf6, avoid the famous endgame with the modest 4.d3: protect e4, keep queens on, play a slow Italian-style game.

variations.name: Steinitz and Schliemann
variations.intro: The old defences: 3...d6 hands over the centre, 3...f5 gambles the king. Sober development refutes both.

variations.name: Noah’s Ark Trap — punish 10.Qxd4?? (…c5 and …c4 snare Bb3)
